title: Comet

infobox.value: is an icy, [small Solar System body](/wiki/Small_Solar_System_body "Small Solar System body") or [interstellar object](/wiki/Interstellar_object "Interstellar object") that warms and begins to release gases when passing close to the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), a process called [outgassing](/wiki/Outgassing "Outgassing"). This produces an extended, gravitationally unbound atmosphere or [coma](/wiki/Coma_(cometary) "Coma (cometary)") surrounding the nucleus, and sometimes a [tail](/wiki/Comet_tail "Comet tail") of gas and dust gas blown out from the coma. These phenomena are due to the effects of [solar radiation](/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation") and the outstreaming [solar wind](/wiki/Solar_wind "Solar wind") plasma acting upon the nucleus of the comet. [Comet nuclei](/wiki/Comet_nuclei "Comet nuclei") range from a few hundred meters to tens of kilometers across and are composed of loose collections of ice, dust, and small rocky particles. The coma may be up to 15 times Earth's diameter, while the tail may stretch beyond one [astronomical unit](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit"). If sufficiently close and bright, a comet may be seen from Earth without the aid of a telescope and can [subtend](/wiki/Subtended_angle "Subtended angle") an arc of up to 30° (60 Moons) across the sky. Comets have been observed and recorded since ancient times by many cultures and religions.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Natural object in space that releases gas

This article is about the astronomical object. For other uses, see [Comet (disambiguation)](/wiki/Comet_(disambiguation) "Comet (disambiguation)").

A **comet** is an icy, [small Solar System body](/wiki/Small_Solar_System_body "Small Solar System body") or [interstellar object](/wiki/Interstellar_object "Interstellar object") that warms and begins to release gases when passing close to the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), a process called [outgassing](/wiki/Outgassing "Outgassing"). This produces an extended, gravitationally unbound atmosphere or [coma](/wiki/Coma_(cometary) "Coma (cometary)") surrounding the nucleus, and sometimes a [tail](/wiki/Comet_tail "Comet tail") of gas and dust gas blown out from the coma. These phenomena are due to the effects of [solar radiation](/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation") and the outstreaming [solar wind](/wiki/Solar_wind "Solar wind") plasma acting upon the nucleus of the comet. [Comet nuclei](/wiki/Comet_nuclei "Comet nuclei") range from a few hundred meters to tens of kilometers across and are composed of loose collections of ice, dust, and small rocky particles. The coma may be up to 15 times Earth's diameter, while the tail may stretch beyond one [astronomical unit](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit"). If sufficiently close and bright, a comet may be seen from Earth without the aid of a telescope and can [subtend](/wiki/Subtended_angle "Subtended angle") an arc of up to 30° (60 Moons) across the sky. Comets have been observed and recorded since ancient times by many cultures and religions.

Comets usually have highly [eccentric](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity") elliptical orbits, and they have a wide range of [orbital periods](/wiki/Orbital_period "Orbital period"), ranging from several years to potentially several millions of years. [Short-period comets](#Short_period) originate in the [Kuiper belt](/wiki/Kuiper_belt "Kuiper belt") or its associated [scattered disc](/wiki/Scattered_disc "Scattered disc"), which lie beyond the orbit of [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune"). [Long-period comets](#Long_period) are thought to originate in the [Oort cloud](/wiki/Oort_cloud "Oort cloud"), a spherical cloud of icy bodies extending from outside the Kuiper belt to halfway to the nearest star.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Long-period comets are set in motion towards the Sun by [gravitational perturbations](/wiki/Gravitational_perturbation "Gravitational perturbation") from [passing stars](/wiki/List_of_nearest_stars#Future_and_past "List of nearest stars") and the [galactic tide](/wiki/Galactic_tide "Galactic tide"). [Hyperbolic comets](/wiki/Hyperbolic_comet "Hyperbolic comet") may pass once through the inner Solar System before being flung to interstellar space. The appearance of a comet is called an apparition.

[Extinct comets](/wiki/Extinct_comet "Extinct comet") that have passed close to the Sun many times have lost nearly all of their [volatile](/wiki/Volatility_(chemistry) "Volatility (chemistry)") ices and dust and may come to resemble small asteroids.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Asteroids are thought to have a different origin from comets, having formed inside the orbit of Jupiter rather than in the outer Solar System.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) However, the discovery of [main-belt comets](/wiki/Main-belt_comet "Main-belt comet") and active [centaur](/wiki/Centaur_(minor_planet) "Centaur (minor planet)") minor planets has blurred the [distinction between asteroids and comets](/wiki/Asteroid#Terminology "Asteroid"). In the early 21st century, the discovery of some minor bodies with long-period comet orbits, but characteristics of inner solar system asteroids, were called [Manx comets](/wiki/Manx_comet "Manx comet"). They are still classified as comets, such as C/2014 S3 (PANSTARRS).[[6]](#cite_note-6) Twenty-seven Manx comets were found from 2013 to 2017.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

As of November 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Comet&action=edit), there are 4,584 known comets.[[8]](#cite_note-8) However, this represents a very small fraction of the total potential comet population, as the reservoir of comet-like bodies in the outer Solar System (in the [Oort cloud](/wiki/Oort_cloud "Oort cloud")) is about one trillion.[[9]](#cite_note-Erickson2003-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Couper2014-10) Roughly one comet per year is visible to the [naked eye](/wiki/Naked_eye "Naked eye"), though many of those are faint and unspectacular.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Particularly bright examples are called "[great comets](/wiki/Great_comet "Great comet")". Comets have been visited by uncrewed probes such as NASA's *[Deep Impact](/wiki/Deep_Impact_(spacecraft) "Deep Impact (spacecraft)")*, which blasted a crater on Comet [Tempel 1](/wiki/Tempel_1 "Tempel 1") to study its interior, and the European Space Agency's *[Rosetta](/wiki/Rosetta_(spacecraft) "Rosetta (spacecraft)")*, which became the first to land a robotic spacecraft on a comet.[[12]](#cite_note-Philae-12)

## Etymology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle_-_cometa_%28British_Library_Cotton_MS_Tiberius_A_VI%2C_folio_10v%29.jpg/250px-Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle_-_cometa_%28British_Library_Cotton_MS_Tiberius_A_VI%2C_folio_10v%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle_-_cometa_(British_Library_Cotton_MS_Tiberius_A_VI,_folio_10v).jpg)

A comet was mentioned in the *[Anglo-Saxon Chronicle](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_Chronicle "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle")* and by the [Venerable Bede](/wiki/Venerable_Bede "Venerable Bede") in the year 729 [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era").[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The word *comet* derives from the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") *cometa* from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *comēta* or *comētēs*. That, in turn, is a [romanization](/wiki/Romanization_of_Greek "Romanization of Greek") of the [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") κομήτης 'wearing long hair', and the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* notes that the term (ἀστὴρ) κομήτης already meant 'long-haired star, comet' in Greek. Κομήτης was derived from κομᾶν (*koman*) 'to wear the hair long', which was itself derived from κόμη (*komē*) 'the hair of the head' and was used to mean 'the tail of a comet'.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The [astronomical symbol](/wiki/Astronomical_symbol "Astronomical symbol") for comets (represented in [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode")) is U+2604 ☄ COMET, consisting of a small disc with three hairlike extensions.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Physical characteristics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Structure_of_a_comet.jpg/250px-Structure_of_a_comet.jpg)](/wiki/File:Structure_of_a_comet.jpg)

Structure of a comet

### Nucleus

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Comet_Hartley_2.jpg/250px-Comet_Hartley_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Comet_Hartley_2.jpg)

Nucleus of [103P/Hartley](/wiki/103P/Hartley "103P/Hartley") as imaged during a [spacecraft flyby](/wiki/Deep_Impact_(spacecraft) "Deep Impact (spacecraft)"). The nucleus is about 2 km in length.

Main article: [Comet nucleus](/wiki/Comet_nucleus "Comet nucleus")

The solid, core structure of a comet is known as the nucleus. Cometary nuclei are composed of an amalgamation of [rock](/wiki/Rock_(geology) "Rock (geology)"), [dust](/wiki/Comet_dust "Comet dust"), [water ice](/wiki/Ice "Ice"), and frozen [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide"), [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide"), [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane"), and [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia").[[18]](#cite_note-18) As such, they are popularly described as "dirty snowballs" after [Fred Whipple](/wiki/Fred_Whipple "Fred Whipple")'s model.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Comets with a higher dust content have been called "icy dirtballs".[[20]](#cite_note-20) The term "icy dirtballs" arose after observation of [Comet 9P/Tempel 1](/wiki/Tempel_1 "Tempel 1") collision with an "impactor" probe sent by NASA Deep Impact mission in July 2005. Research conducted in 2014 suggests that comets are like "[deep fried ice cream](/wiki/Fried_ice_cream "Fried ice cream")", in that their surfaces are formed of dense crystalline ice mixed with [organic compounds](/wiki/Organic_compound "Organic compound"), while the interior ice is colder and less dense.[[21]](#cite_note-NASA-20150210-21)

The surface of the nucleus is generally dry, dusty or rocky, suggesting that the ices are hidden beneath a surface crust several metres thick. Nuclei contain a variety of organic compounds, which may include [methanol](/wiki/Methanol "Methanol"), [hydrogen cyanide](/wiki/Hydrogen_cyanide "Hydrogen cyanide"), [formaldehyde](/wiki/Formaldehyde "Formaldehyde"), [ethanol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol"), [ethane](/wiki/Ethane "Ethane"), and perhaps more complex molecules such as long-chain [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") and [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid").[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 2009, it was confirmed that the amino acid [glycine](/wiki/Glycine "Glycine") had been found in the comet dust recovered by NASA's [Stardust mission](/wiki/Stardust_(spacecraft) "Stardust (spacecraft)").[[24]](#cite_note-24) In August 2011, a report, based on [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") studies of [meteorites](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite") found on Earth, was published suggesting [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") and [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA") components ([adenine](/wiki/Adenine "Adenine"), [guanine](/wiki/Guanine "Guanine"), and related organic molecules) may have been formed on [asteroids](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid") and comets.[[25]](#cite_note-Callahan-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Steigerwald-26)

The outer surfaces of cometary nuclei have a very low [albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo"), making them among the least reflective objects found in the Solar System. The [Giotto](/wiki/Giotto_(spacecraft) "Giotto (spacecraft)") [space probe](/wiki/Space_probe "Space probe") found that the nucleus of [Halley's Comet](/wiki/Halley%27s_Comet "Halley's Comet") (1P/Halley) reflects about four percent of the light that falls on it,[[27]](#cite_note-dark-27) and [Deep Space 1](/wiki/Deep_Space_1 "Deep Space 1") discovered that [Comet Borrelly](/wiki/19P/Borrelly "19P/Borrelly")'s surface reflects less than 3.0%;[[27]](#cite_note-dark-27) by comparison, [asphalt](/wiki/Bitumen "Bitumen") reflects seven percent. The dark surface material of the nucleus may consist of complex organic compounds. Solar heating drives off lighter [volatile](/wiki/Volatility_(chemistry) "Volatility (chemistry)") [compounds](/wiki/Chemical_compound "Chemical compound"), leaving behind larger organic compounds that tend to be very dark, like [tar](/wiki/Tar "Tar") or [crude oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"). The low reflectivity of cometary surfaces causes them to absorb the heat that drives their [outgassing](/wiki/Outgassing "Outgassing") processes.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Comet nuclei with radii of up to 30 kilometers (19 mi) have been observed,[[29]](#cite_note-29) but ascertaining their exact size is difficult.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The nucleus of [322P/SOHO](/wiki/322P/SOHO "322P/SOHO") is probably only 100–200 meters (330–660 ft) in diameter.[[31]](#cite_note-soho1-31) A lack of smaller comets being detected despite the increased sensitivity of instruments has led some to suggest that there is a real lack of comets smaller than 100 meters (330 ft) across.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Known comets have been estimated to have an average density of 0.6 g/cm3 (0.35 oz/cu in).[[33]](#cite_note-Britt2006-33) Because of their low mass, comet nuclei do not [become spherical](/wiki/Gravitational_collapse "Gravitational collapse") under their own [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") and therefore have irregular shapes.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Comet_wild_2.jpg/250px-Comet_wild_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Comet_wild_2.jpg)

Comet [81P/Wild](/wiki/81P/Wild "81P/Wild") exhibits jets on light side and dark side, stark relief, and is dry.

Roughly six percent of the [near-Earth asteroids](/wiki/Near-Earth_asteroid "Near-Earth asteroid") are thought to be the [extinct nuclei of comets](/wiki/Extinct_comet "Extinct comet") that no longer experience outgassing,[[35]](#cite_note-dormant-35) including [14827 Hypnos](/wiki/14827_Hypnos "14827 Hypnos") and [3552 Don Quixote](/wiki/3552_Don_Quixote "3552 Don Quixote").

Results from the [*Rosetta*](/wiki/Rosetta_(spacecraft) "Rosetta (spacecraft)") and [*Philae*](/wiki/Philae_(spacecraft) "Philae (spacecraft)") spacecraft show that the nucleus of [67P/Churyumov–Gerasimenko](/wiki/67P/Churyumov%E2%80%93Gerasimenko "67P/Churyumov–Gerasimenko") has no magnetic field, which suggests that magnetism may not have played a role in the early formation of [planetesimals](/wiki/Planetesimal "Planetesimal").[[36]](#cite_note-esa20150414-36)[[37]](#cite_note-nature20150414-37) Further, the [ALICE spectrograph](/wiki/Rosetta_(spacecraft)#Instruments "Rosetta (spacecraft)") on *Rosetta* determined that [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron") (within 1 km (0.62 mi) above the [comet nucleus](/wiki/Comet_nucleus "Comet nucleus")) produced from [photoionization](/wiki/Photoionization "Photoionization") of water molecules by [solar radiation](/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight"), and not [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon") from the Sun as thought earlier, are responsible for the degradation of water and [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") [molecules](/wiki/Molecule "Molecule") released from the comet nucleus into its coma.[[38]](#cite_note-NASA-20150602-38)[[39]](#cite_note-AA-20150602-39) Instruments on the *Philae* lander found at least sixteen organic compounds at the comet's surface, four of which ([acetamide](/wiki/Acetamide "Acetamide"), [acetone](/wiki/Acetone "Acetone"), [methyl isocyanate](/wiki/Methyl_isocyanate "Methyl isocyanate") and [propionaldehyde](/wiki/Propionaldehyde "Propionaldehyde")) have been detected for the first time on a comet.[[40]](#cite_note-wapo20150730-40)[[41]](#cite_note-esa20150730-41)[[42]](#cite_note-SCI-20150731-42)

### Coma

Main article: [Coma (comet)](/wiki/Coma_(comet) "Coma (comet)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Hubble%27s_Last_Look_at_Comet_ISON_Before_Perihelion.jpg/250px-Hubble%27s_Last_Look_at_Comet_ISON_Before_Perihelion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hubble%27s_Last_Look_at_Comet_ISON_Before_Perihelion.jpg)

[Hubble](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope") image of [Comet ISON](/wiki/Comet_ISON "Comet ISON") shortly before [perihelion](/wiki/Perihelion "Perihelion").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Comet_borrelly.jpg/250px-Comet_borrelly.jpg)](/wiki/File:Comet_borrelly.jpg)

[Comet Borrelly](/wiki/19P/Borrelly "19P/Borrelly") exhibits jets, but has no surface ice.

The streams of dust and gas thus released form a huge and extremely thin atmosphere around the comet called the "coma". The force exerted on the coma by the Sun's [radiation pressure](/wiki/Radiation_pressure "Radiation pressure") and [solar wind](/wiki/Solar_wind "Solar wind") cause an enormous "tail" to form pointing away from the Sun.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The coma is generally made of water and dust, with water making up to 90% of the [volatiles](/wiki/Volatile_(astrogeology) "Volatile (astrogeology)") that outflow from the nucleus when the comet is within 3 to 4 [AU](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit") (450 to 600 million km; 280 to 370 million mi) of the Sun.[[52]](#cite_note-Combi2004-52) The [H2O](/wiki/Water "Water") parent molecule is destroyed primarily through [photodissociation](/wiki/Photodissociation "Photodissociation") and to a much smaller extent [photoionization](/wiki/Photoionization "Photoionization"), with the solar wind playing a minor role in the destruction of water compared to [photochemistry](/wiki/Photochemistry "Photochemistry").[[52]](#cite_note-Combi2004-52) Larger dust particles are left along the comet's orbital path whereas smaller particles are pushed away from the Sun into the comet's tail by [light pressure](/wiki/Radiation_pressure "Radiation pressure").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Although the solid nucleus of comets is generally less than 60 kilometers (37 mi) across, the coma may be thousands or millions of kilometers across, sometimes becoming larger than the Sun.[[54]](#cite_note-54) For example, about a month after an outburst in October 2007, comet [17P/Holmes](/wiki/17P/Holmes "17P/Holmes") briefly had a tenuous dust atmosphere larger than the Sun.[[55]](#cite_note-atmosphere2-55) The [Great Comet of 1811](/wiki/Great_Comet_of_1811 "Great Comet of 1811") had a coma roughly the diameter of the Sun.[[56]](#cite_note-primer-56) Even though the coma can become quite large, its size can decrease about the time it crosses the orbit of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") around 1.5 [AU](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit") (220 million km; 140 million mi) from the Sun.[[56]](#cite_note-primer-56) At this distance the solar wind becomes strong enough to blow the gas and dust away from the coma, and in doing so enlarging the tail.[[56]](#cite_note-primer-56) Ion tails have been observed to extend one astronomical unit (150 million km) or more.[[55]](#cite_note-atmosphere2-55)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/PIA20119-CometChristensen-C2006W3-CO2-WISE-20100420.jpg/250px-PIA20119-CometChristensen-C2006W3-CO2-WISE-20100420.jpg)](/wiki/File:PIA20119-CometChristensen-C2006W3-CO2-WISE-20100420.jpg)

C/2006 W3 (Christensen) emitting carbon gas (IR image)

Both the coma and tail are illuminated by the Sun and may become visible when a comet passes through the inner Solar System, the dust reflects sunlight directly while the gases glow from [ionisation](/wiki/Ion "Ion").[[57]](#cite_note-le-57) Most comets are too faint to be visible without the aid of a [telescope](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope"), but a few each decade become bright enough to be visible to the naked eye.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Occasionally a comet may experience a huge and sudden outburst of gas and dust, during which the size of the coma greatly increases for a period of time. This happened in 2007 to [Comet Holmes](/wiki/17P/Holmes "17P/Holmes").[[59]](#cite_note-atmosphere1-59)

In 1996, comets were found to emit [X-rays](/wiki/X-ray "X-ray").[[60]](#cite_note-60) This greatly surprised astronomers because X-ray emission is usually associated with very [high-temperature bodies](/wiki/Black-body_radiation "Black-body radiation"). [Thomas E. Cravens](/wiki/Thomas_E._Cravens "Thomas E. Cravens") was the first to propose an explanation in early 1997.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The X-rays are generated by the interaction between comets and the solar wind: when highly charged solar wind ions fly through a cometary atmosphere, they collide with cometary atoms and molecules, "stealing" one or more electrons from the atom in a process called "charge exchange". This exchange or transfer of an electron to the solar wind ion is followed by its de-excitation into the ground state of the ion by the emission of X-rays and [far ultraviolet](/wiki/Far_ultraviolet "Far ultraviolet") photons.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Bow shock

[Bow shocks](/wiki/Bow_shock "Bow shock") form as a result of the interaction between the solar wind and the cometary ionosphere, which is created by the ionization of gases in the coma. As the comet approaches the Sun, increasing outgassing rates cause the coma to expand, and the sunlight ionizes gases in the coma. When the solar wind passes through this ion coma, the bow shock appears.

The first observations were made in the 1980s and 1990s as several spacecraft flew by comets [21P/Giacobini–Zinner](/wiki/21P/Giacobini%E2%80%93Zinner "21P/Giacobini–Zinner"),[[63]](#cite_note-63) 1P/Halley,[[64]](#cite_note-64) and [26P/Grigg–Skjellerup](/wiki/26P/Grigg%E2%80%93Skjellerup "26P/Grigg–Skjellerup").[[65]](#cite_note-65) It was then found that the bow shocks at comets are wider and more gradual than the sharp planetary bow shocks seen at, for example, Earth. These observations were all made near [perihelion](/wiki/Perihelion "Perihelion") when the bow shocks already were fully developed.

The [*Rosetta*](/wiki/Rosetta_(spacecraft) "Rosetta (spacecraft)") spacecraft observed the bow shock at comet [67P/Churyumov–Gerasimenko](/wiki/67P/Churyumov%E2%80%93Gerasimenko "67P/Churyumov–Gerasimenko") at an early stage of bow shock development when the outgassing increased during the comet's journey toward the Sun. This young bow shock was called the "infant bow shock". The infant bow shock is asymmetric and, relative to the distance to the nucleus, wider than fully developed bow shocks.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### Tails

Main article: [Comet tail](/wiki/Comet_tail "Comet tail")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Cometorbit01.svg/330px-Cometorbit01.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cometorbit01.svg)

Typical direction of tails during a comet's orbit near the Sun

In the outer [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"), comets remain frozen and inactive and are extremely difficult or impossible to detect from Earth due to their small size. Statistical detections of inactive comet nuclei in the [Kuiper belt](/wiki/Kuiper_belt "Kuiper belt") have been reported from observations by the [Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope")[[67]](#cite_note-Cochran1995-67)[[68]](#cite_note-Cochran1998-68) but these detections have been questioned.[[69]](#cite_note-Brown1997-69)[[70]](#cite_note-Jewitt1996-70) As a comet approaches the inner Solar System, [solar radiation](/wiki/Solar_radiation "Solar radiation") causes the volatile materials within the comet to vaporize and stream out of the nucleus, carrying dust away with them.

The streams of dust and gas each form their own distinct tail, pointing in slightly different directions. The tail of dust is left behind near the comet's orbit in such a manner that it often forms a curved tail called the type II or dust tail.[[57]](#cite_note-le-57) At the same time, the ion or type I tail, made of gases, always points directly away from the Sun because this gas is more strongly affected by the solar wind than is dust, following magnetic field lines rather than an orbital trajectory.[[71]](#cite_note-71) On occasions—such as when Earth passes through a comet's orbital plane, the [antitail](/wiki/Anti-tail "Anti-tail"), pointing in the opposite direction to the ion and dust tails, may be seen.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Comet_Parts.svg/250px-Comet_Parts.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Comet_Parts.svg)

Diagram of a comet showing the [dust trail](/wiki/Anti-tail "Anti-tail"), the dust tail, and the ion gas tail formed by [solar wind](/wiki/Solar_wind "Solar wind")

The observation of antitails contributed significantly to the discovery of solar wind.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The ion tail is formed as a result of the ionization by solar ultra-violet radiation of particles in the coma. Once the particles have been ionized, they attain a net positive electrical charge, which in turn gives rise to an "induced [magnetosphere](/wiki/Magnetosphere "Magnetosphere")" around the comet. The comet and its induced magnetic field form an obstacle to outward flowing solar wind particles. Because the relative orbital speed of the comet and the solar wind is supersonic, a [bow shock](/wiki/Bow_shock "Bow shock") is formed upstream of the comet in the flow direction of the solar wind. In this bow shock, large concentrations of cometary ions (called "pick-up ions") congregate and act to "load" the solar magnetic field with plasma, such that the field lines "drape" around the comet forming the ion tail.[[74]](#cite_note-pp_864-74)

If the ion tail loading is sufficient, the magnetic field lines are squeezed together to the point where, at some distance along the ion tail, [magnetic reconnection](/wiki/Magnetic_reconnection "Magnetic reconnection") occurs. This leads to a "tail disconnection event".[[74]](#cite_note-pp_864-74) This has been observed on a number of occasions, one notable event being recorded on 20 April 2007, when the ion tail of [Encke's Comet](/wiki/Encke%27s_Comet "Encke's Comet") was completely severed while the comet passed through a [coronal mass ejection](/wiki/Coronal_mass_ejection "Coronal mass ejection"). This event was observed by the [STEREO space probe](/wiki/STEREO "STEREO").[[75]](#cite_note-75)

In 2013, [ESA](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") scientists reported that the [ionosphere](/wiki/Ionosphere "Ionosphere") of the planet [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") streams outwards in a manner similar to the ion tail seen streaming from a comet under similar conditions."[[76]](#cite_note-ESA-20130129-76)[[77]](#cite_note-Space-20130130-77)

### Jets

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Hartley2jets2_epoxi_big.jpg/250px-Hartley2jets2_epoxi_big.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hartley2jets2_epoxi_big.jpg)

Gas and snow jets of [103P/Hartley](/wiki/103P/Hartley "103P/Hartley")

Uneven heating can cause newly generated gases to break out of a weak spot on the surface of comet's nucleus, like a geyser.[[78]](#cite_note-jets-78) These streams of gas and dust can cause the nucleus to spin, and even split apart.[[78]](#cite_note-jets-78) In 2010 it was revealed that [sublimation](/wiki/Sublimation_(phase_transition) "Sublimation (phase transition)") of [dry ice](/wiki/Dry_ice "Dry ice") (frozen carbon dioxide) can power jets of material flowing out of a comet nucleus.[[79]](#cite_note-79) Infrared imaging of Hartley 2 shows such jets exiting and carrying with it dust grains into the coma.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

## Orbital characteristics

Most comets are [small Solar System bodies](/wiki/Small_Solar_System_body "Small Solar System body") with elongated [elliptical orbits](/wiki/Elliptical_orbit "Elliptical orbit") that take them close to the Sun for a part of their orbit and then out into the further reaches of the Solar System for the remainder.[[81]](#cite_note-81) Comets are often classified according to the length of their [orbital periods](/wiki/Orbital_period "Orbital period"): The longer the period the more elongated the ellipse.

### Short period

Main articles: [List of numbered comets](/wiki/List_of_numbered_comets "List of numbered comets") and [List of Halley-type comets](/wiki/List_of_Halley-type_comets "List of Halley-type comets")

[Periodic comets](/wiki/List_of_periodic_comets "List of periodic comets") or short-period comets are generally defined as those having orbital periods of less than 200 years.[[82]](#cite_note-82) They usually orbit more-or-less in the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic") plane in the same direction as the planets.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Their orbits typically take them out to the region of the outer planets ([Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") and beyond) at [aphelion](/wiki/Aphelion "Aphelion"); for example, the aphelion of Halley's Comet is a little beyond the orbit of [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune"). Comets whose aphelia are near a major planet's orbit are called its "family".[[84]](#cite_note-Wilson1909-84) Such families are thought to arise from the planet capturing formerly long-period comets into shorter orbits.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

At the shorter orbital period extreme, Encke's Comet has an orbit that does not reach the orbit of Jupiter, and is known as an **Encke-type comet**. Short-period comets with orbital periods less than 20 years and low inclinations (up to 30 degrees) to the ecliptic are called traditional **Jupiter-family comets** (JFCs).[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-britastro-87) Those like Halley, with orbital periods of between 20 and 200 years and inclinations extending from zero to more than 90 degrees, are called **Halley-type comets** (HTCs).[[88]](#cite_note-Morbidelli2006-88)[[89]](#cite_note-jewitt2002-89) As of July 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Comet&action=edit) there are 74 known Encke-type comets (six of which are classified as [near-earth objects](/wiki/Near-earth_object "Near-earth object") (NEOs)), 109 HTCs (36 of which are NEOs), and 815 JFCs (153 of which are NEOs).[[90]](#cite_note-SSD-JPL-90)

Recently discovered [main-belt comets](/wiki/Main-belt_comet "Main-belt comet") form a distinct class, orbiting in more circular orbits within the [asteroid belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt").[[91]](#cite_note-NYT-20221118-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

Because their elliptical orbits frequently take them close to the giant planets, comets are subject to further [gravitational perturbations](/wiki/Perturbation_(astronomy) "Perturbation (astronomy)").[[93]](#cite_note-93) Short-period comets have a tendency for their aphelia to coincide with a [giant planet](/wiki/Giant_planet "Giant planet")'s semi-major axis, with the JFCs being the largest group.[[87]](#cite_note-britastro-87) It is clear that comets coming in from the [Oort cloud](/wiki/Oort_cloud "Oort cloud") often have their orbits strongly influenced by the gravity of giant planets as a result of a close encounter. Jupiter is the source of the greatest perturbations, being more than twice as massive as all the other planets combined. These perturbations can deflect long-period comets into shorter orbital periods.[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)

Based on their orbital characteristics, short-period comets are thought to originate from the [centaurs](/wiki/Centaur_(minor_planet) "Centaur (minor planet)") and the Kuiper belt/[scattered disc](/wiki/Scattered_disc "Scattered disc")[[96]](#cite_note-96) —a disk of objects in the trans-Neptunian region—whereas the source of long-period comets is thought to be the far more distant spherical Oort cloud (after the Dutch astronomer [Jan Hendrik Oort](/wiki/Jan_Hendrik_Oort "Jan Hendrik Oort") who hypothesized its existence).[[97]](#cite_note-97) Vast swarms of comet-like bodies are thought to orbit the Sun in these distant regions in roughly circular orbits. Occasionally the gravitational influence of the outer planets (in the case of Kuiper belt objects) or nearby stars (in the case of Oort cloud objects) may throw one of these bodies into an elliptical orbit that takes it inwards toward the Sun to form a visible comet. Unlike the return of periodic comets, whose orbits have been established by previous observations, the appearance of new comets by this mechanism is unpredictable.[[98]](#cite_note-98) When flung into the orbit of the sun, and being continuously dragged towards it, tons of matter are stripped from the comets which greatly influence their lifetime; the more stripped, the shorter they live and vice versa.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

### Long period

See also: [List of long-period comets](/wiki/List_of_long-period_comets "List of long-period comets"), [List of near-parabolic comets](/wiki/List_of_near-parabolic_comets "List of near-parabolic comets"), and [List of hyperbolic comets](/wiki/List_of_hyperbolic_comets "List of hyperbolic comets")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Comet_Kohoutek_orbit_p391.svg/250px-Comet_Kohoutek_orbit_p391.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Comet_Kohoutek_orbit_p391.svg)

Orbits of [Comet Kohoutek](/wiki/Comet_Kohoutek "Comet Kohoutek") (red) and Earth (blue), illustrating the high [eccentricity](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity") of its orbit and its rapid motion when close to the Sun

Long-period comets have highly [eccentric](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity") orbits and periods ranging from 200 years to thousands or even millions of years.[[100]](#cite_note-SBP-100) The future orbit of a long-period comet is properly obtained when the [osculating orbit](/wiki/Osculating_orbit "Osculating orbit") is computed at an epoch after leaving the planetary region and is calculated with respect to the [center of mass of the Solar System](/wiki/Barycentric_coordinates_(astronomy) "Barycentric coordinates (astronomy)"). An eccentricity greater than 1 when near perihelion does not necessarily mean that a comet will leave the Solar System.[[101]](#cite_note-Elenin2011-101) For example, [Comet McNaught](/wiki/C/2006_P1 "C/2006 P1") had a heliocentric osculating eccentricity of 1.000019 near its perihelion passage [epoch](/wiki/Epoch_(astronomy) "Epoch (astronomy)") in January 2007 but is bound to the Sun with roughly a 92,600-year orbit because the [eccentricity](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity") drops below 1 as it moves farther from the Sun.

By definition long-period comets remain gravitationally bound to the Sun; those comets that are ejected from the Solar System due to close passes by major planets are no longer properly considered as having "periods". The orbits of long-period comets take them far beyond the outer planets at aphelia, and the plane of their orbits need not lie near the ecliptic. Long-period comets such as [C/1999 F1](/wiki/C/1999_F1 "C/1999 F1") and [C/2017 T2 (PANSTARRS)](/wiki/C/2017_T2_(PANSTARRS) "C/2017 T2 (PANSTARRS)") can have aphelion distances of nearly 70,000 AU (0.34 pc; 1.1 ly) with orbital periods estimated around 6 million years.

Single-apparition or non-periodic comets are similar to long-period comets because they have [parabolic](/wiki/Parabolic_trajectory "Parabolic trajectory") or slightly [hyperbolic](/wiki/Hyperbolic_trajectory "Hyperbolic trajectory") trajectories[[100]](#cite_note-SBP-100) when near perihelion in the inner Solar System. However, gravitational perturbations from giant planets cause their orbits to change. Single-apparition comets have a hyperbolic or parabolic [osculating orbit](/wiki/Osculating_orbit "Osculating orbit") which allows them to permanently exit the Solar System after a single pass of the Sun.[[102]](#cite_note-102) The Sun's [Hill sphere](/wiki/Hill_sphere "Hill sphere") has an [unstable](/wiki/Unstable "Unstable") maximum boundary of 230,000 AU (1.1 pc; 3.6 ly).[[103]](#cite_note-Chebotarev1964-103) Only a few hundred comets have been seen to reach a hyperbolic orbit (e > 1) when near perihelion[[104]](#cite_note-e1-104) that using a heliocentric unperturbed [two-body](/wiki/Two-body_problem "Two-body problem") [best-fit](/wiki/Curve_fitting "Curve fitting") suggests they may escape the Solar System.

As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Comet&action=edit), three objects have been discovered with an [eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(mathematics) "Eccentricity (mathematics)") significantly greater than one: [1I/ʻOumuamua](/wiki/1I/%CA%BBOumuamua "1I/ʻOumuamua"), [2I/Borisov](/wiki/2I/Borisov "2I/Borisov"), and [3I/ATLAS](/wiki/3I/ATLAS "3I/ATLAS"), indicating an origin outside the Solar System. While ʻOumuamua, with an eccentricity of about 1.2, showed no optical signs of cometary activity during its passage through the inner Solar System in October 2017, changes to its trajectory—which suggests [outgassing](/wiki/Outgassing "Outgassing")—indicate that it is probably a comet.[[105]](#cite_note-Oumuamua-105) On the other hand, 2I/Borisov, with an estimated eccentricity of about 3.36, has been observed to have the coma feature of comets, and is considered the first detected [interstellar comet](/wiki/Interstellar_object "Interstellar object").[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-cnn-107) 3I/ATLAS has an eccentricity of about 6.1, and also has a coma, indicating that it is also a comet. Comet [C/1980 E1](/wiki/C/1980_E1 "C/1980 E1") had an orbital period of roughly 7.1 million years before the 1982 perihelion passage, but a 1980 encounter with Jupiter accelerated the comet giving it the largest eccentricity (1.057) of any known solar comet with a reasonable observation arc.[[108]](#cite_note-C/1980E1-jpl-108) Comets not expected to return to the inner Solar System include [C/1980 E1](/wiki/C/1980_E1 "C/1980 E1"), [C/2000 U5](/wiki/C/2000_U5 "C/2000 U5"), [C/2001 Q4 (NEAT)](/wiki/C/2001_Q4_(NEAT) "C/2001 Q4 (NEAT)"), [C/2009 R1](/wiki/C/2009_R1 "C/2009 R1"), [C/1956 R1](/wiki/C/1956_R1 "C/1956 R1"), and [C/2007 F1](/wiki/C/2007_F1 "C/2007 F1") (LONEOS).

Some authorities use the term "periodic comet" to refer to any comet with a periodic orbit (that is, all short-period comets plus all long-period comets),[[109]](#cite_note-109) whereas others use it to mean exclusively short-period comets.[[100]](#cite_note-SBP-100) Similarly, although the literal meaning of "non-periodic comet" is the same as "single-apparition comet", some use it to mean all comets that are not "periodic" in the second sense (that is, to include all comets with a period greater than 200 years).

Early observations have revealed a few genuinely hyperbolic (i.e. non-periodic) trajectories, but no more than could be accounted for by perturbations from Jupiter. Comets from [interstellar space](/wiki/Interstellar_space "Interstellar space") are moving with velocities of the same order as the relative velocities of stars near the Sun (a few tens of km per second). When such objects enter the Solar System, they have a positive [specific orbital energy](/wiki/Specific_orbital_energy "Specific orbital energy") resulting in a positive [velocity at infinity](/wiki/Hyperbolic_excess_velocity "Hyperbolic excess velocity") (![{\displaystyle v_{\infty }\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4439fe0f5c56e071edb64af0bd2c916211475fe4)) and have notably hyperbolic trajectories. A rough calculation shows that there might be four hyperbolic comets per century within Jupiter's orbit, give or take one and perhaps two orders of [magnitude](/wiki/Order_of_magnitude "Order of magnitude").[[110]](#cite_note-110)

[Hyperbolic comet](/wiki/Hyperbolic_comet "Hyperbolic comet") discoveries[[111]](#cite_note-e1-name-111)

### Oort cloud and Hills cloud

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Small_objects_in_the_Solar_System_ESA25188647.jpg/500px-Small_objects_in_the_Solar_System_ESA25188647.jpg)](/wiki/File:Small_objects_in_the_Solar_System_ESA25188647.jpg)

The [Oort cloud](/wiki/Oort_cloud "Oort cloud") thought to surround the Solar System. Showed with Kuiper Belt and Asteroid Belt for comparison.

Main articles: [Oort cloud](/wiki/Oort_cloud "Oort cloud") and [Hills cloud](/wiki/Hills_cloud "Hills cloud")

The Oort cloud is thought to occupy a vast space starting from between 2,000 and 5,000 AU (0.03 and 0.08 ly)[[112]](#cite_note-book-112) to as far as 50,000 AU (0.79 ly)[[88]](#cite_note-Morbidelli2006-88) from the Sun. This cloud encases the celestial bodies that start at the middle of the Solar System—the Sun, all the way to outer limits of the Kuiper Belt. The Oort cloud consists of viable materials necessary for the creation of celestial bodies. The Solar System's planets exist only because of the planetesimals (chunks of leftover space that assisted in the creation of planets) that were condensed and formed by the gravity of the Sun. The eccentric made from these trapped planetesimals is why the Oort Cloud even exists.[[113]](#cite_note-113) Some estimates place the outer edge at between 100,000 and 200,000 AU (1.58 and 3.16 ly).[[112]](#cite_note-book-112) The region can be subdivided into a spherical outer Oort cloud of 20,000–50,000 AU (0.32–0.79 ly), and a doughnut-shaped inner cloud, the Hills cloud, of 2,000–20,000 AU (0.03–0.32 ly).[[114]](#cite_note-114) The outer cloud is only weakly bound to the Sun and supplies the long-period (and possibly Halley-type) comets that fall to inside the orbit of [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune").[[88]](#cite_note-Morbidelli2006-88) The inner Oort cloud is also known as the Hills cloud, named after [Jack G. Hills](/wiki/Jack_G._Hills "Jack G. Hills"), who proposed its existence in 1981.[[115]](#cite_note-hills1981-115) Models predict that the inner cloud should have tens or hundreds of times as many cometary nuclei as the outer halo;[[115]](#cite_note-hills1981-115)[[116]](#cite_note-levison2001-116)[[117]](#cite_note-Donahue1991-117) it is seen as a possible source of new comets that resupply the relatively tenuous outer cloud as the latter's numbers are gradually depleted. The Hills cloud explains the continued existence of the Oort cloud after billions of years.[[118]](#cite_note-Julio1997-118)

### Exocomets

Main article: [Exocomet](/wiki/Exocomet "Exocomet")

[Exocomets](/wiki/Exocomet "Exocomet") beyond the Solar System have been detected and may be common in the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way").[[119]](#cite_note-berk-119) The first exocomet system detected was around [Beta Pictoris](/wiki/Beta_Pictoris "Beta Pictoris"), a very young [A-type main-sequence star](/wiki/A-type_main-sequence_star "A-type main-sequence star"), in 1987.[[120]](#cite_note-Space-20130107-120)[[121]](#cite_note-Beust1990-121) A total of 11 such exocomet systems have been identified as of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Comet&action=edit), using the [absorption spectrum](/wiki/Absorption_spectroscopy "Absorption spectroscopy") caused by the large clouds of gas emitted by comets when passing close to their star.[[119]](#cite_note-berk-119)[[120]](#cite_note-Space-20130107-120) For ten years the [Kepler space telescope](/wiki/Kepler_space_telescope "Kepler space telescope") was responsible for searching for planets and other forms outside of the solar system. The first transiting exocomets were found in February 2018 by a group consisting of professional astronomers and [citizen scientists](/wiki/Citizen_science "Citizen science") in light curves recorded by the Kepler Space Telescope.[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123) After Kepler Space Telescope retired in October 2018, a new telescope called TESS Telescope has taken over Kepler's mission. Since the launch of TESS, astronomers have discovered the transits of comets around the star Beta Pictoris using a light curve from TESS.[[124]](#cite_note-124)[[125]](#cite_note-125) Since TESS has taken over, astronomers have since been able to better distinguish exocomets with the spectroscopic method. New planets are detected by the white light curve method which is viewed as a symmetrical dip in the charts readings when a planet overshadows its parent star. However, after further evaluation of these light curves, it has been discovered that the asymmetrical patterns of the dips presented are caused by the tail of a comet or of hundreds of comets.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

## Effects of comets

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/PSM_V18_D201_Shower_of_perseids_sept_6_and_7.jpg/250px-PSM_V18_D201_Shower_of_perseids_sept_6_and_7.jpg)](/wiki/File:PSM_V18_D201_Shower_of_perseids_sept_6_and_7.jpg)

Diagram of [Perseid meteors](/wiki/Perseids "Perseids")

### Connection to meteor showers

As a comet is heated during close passes to the Sun, [outgassing](/wiki/Outgassing "Outgassing") of its icy components releases solid debris too large to be swept away by [radiation pressure](/wiki/Radiation_pressure "Radiation pressure") and the solar wind.[[127]](#cite_note-127) If Earth's orbit sends it through that trail of debris, which is composed mostly of fine grains of rocky material, there is likely to be a [meteor shower](/wiki/Meteor_shower "Meteor shower") as Earth passes through. Denser trails of debris produce quick but intense meteor showers and less dense trails create longer but less intense showers. Typically, the density of the debris trail is related to how long ago the parent comet released the material.[[128]](#cite_note-sciam19990920-128)[[129]](#cite_note-natgeo20190207-129) The [Perseid meteor shower](/wiki/Perseids "Perseids"), for example, occurs every year between 9 and 13 August, when Earth passes through the orbit of [Comet Swift–Tuttle](/wiki/Comet_Swift%E2%80%93Tuttle "Comet Swift–Tuttle"). Halley's Comet is the source of the [Orionid shower](/wiki/Orionids "Orionids") in October.[[130]](#cite_note-showers-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131)

### Comets and impact on life

Many comets and asteroids collided with Earth in its early stages. Many scientists think that comets bombarding the young Earth about 4 billion years ago brought the [vast quantities of water](/wiki/Origin_of_water_on_Earth "Origin of water on Earth") that now fill Earth's oceans, or at least a significant portion of it. Others have cast doubt on this idea.[[132]](#cite_note-132) The detection of organic molecules, including [polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons](/wiki/Polycyclic_aromatic_hydrocarbon "Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon"),[[21]](#cite_note-NASA-20150210-21) in significant quantities in comets has led to speculation that comets or [meteorites](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite") may have brought the precursors of life—or even life itself—to Earth.[[133]](#cite_note-133) In 2013 it was suggested that impacts between rocky and icy surfaces, such as comets, had the potential to create the [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") that make up [proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") through [shock synthesis](/wiki/Shock_synthesis "Shock synthesis").[[134]](#cite_note-134) The speed at which the comets entered the atmosphere, combined with the magnitude of energy created after initial contact, allowed smaller molecules to condense into the larger macro-molecules that served as the foundation for life.[[135]](#cite_note-135) In 2015, scientists found significant amounts of molecular oxygen in the outgassings of comet 67P, suggesting that the molecule may occur more often than had been thought, and thus less an indicator of life as has been supposed.[[136]](#cite_note-136)

It is suspected that comet impacts have, over long timescales, delivered significant quantities of water to Earth's [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), some of which may have survived as [lunar ice](/wiki/Lunar_ice "Lunar ice").[[137]](#cite_note-137) Comet and [meteoroid](/wiki/Meteoroid "Meteoroid") impacts are thought to be responsible for the existence of [tektites](/wiki/Tektite "Tektite") and [australites](/wiki/Australite "Australite").[[138]](#cite_note-138)

### Fear of comets

Fear of comets as [acts of God](/wiki/Act_of_God "Act of God") and signs of impending doom was highest in Europe from 1200 to 1650 [CE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era").[[139]](#cite_note-ley196710-139) The year after the [Great Comet of 1618](/wiki/Great_Comet_of_1618 "Great Comet of 1618"), for example, [Gotthard Arthusius](/wiki/Gotthard_Arthusius "Gotthard Arthusius") published a pamphlet stating that it was a sign that the [Day of Judgment](/wiki/Day_of_Judgment "Day of Judgment") was near.[[140]](#cite_note-140) He listed ten pages of comet-related disasters, including "earthquakes, floods, changes in river courses, hail storms, hot and dry weather, poor harvests, epidemics, war and treason and high prices".[[139]](#cite_note-ley196710-139)

By 1700 most scholars concluded that such events occurred whether a comet was seen or not. Using [Edmond Halley](/wiki/Edmond_Halley "Edmond Halley")'s records of comet sightings, however, [William Whiston](/wiki/William_Whiston "William Whiston") in 1711 wrote that the [Great Comet of 1680](/wiki/Great_Comet_of_1680 "Great Comet of 1680") had a periodicity of 574 years and was responsible for the [worldwide flood in the Book of Genesis](/wiki/Genesis_flood_narrative "Genesis flood narrative"), by pouring water on Earth. His announcement revived for another century fear of comets, now as direct threats to the world instead of signs of disasters.[[139]](#cite_note-ley196710-139) Spectroscopic analysis in 1910 found the toxic gas [cyanogen](/wiki/Cyanogen "Cyanogen") in the tail of Halley's Comet,[[141]](#cite_note-141) causing panicked buying of gas masks and quack "anti-comet pills" and "anti-comet umbrellas" by the public.[[142]](#cite_note-142)

## Fate of comets

### Departure (ejection) from Solar System

If a comet is traveling fast enough, it may leave the Solar System. Such comets follow the open path of a hyperbola, and as such, they are called hyperbolic comets. Solar comets are only known to be ejected by [interacting](/wiki/Perturbation_(astronomy) "Perturbation (astronomy)") with another object in the Solar System, such as Jupiter.[[143]](#cite_note-143) An example of this is Comet [C/1980 E1](/wiki/C/1980_E1 "C/1980 E1"), which was shifted from an orbit of 7.1 million years around the Sun, to a hyperbolic trajectory, after a 1980 close pass by the planet Jupiter.[[144]](#cite_note-barycenter-144) Interstellar comets such as [1I/ʻOumuamua](/wiki/1I/%CA%BBOumuamua "1I/ʻOumuamua"), [2I/Borisov](/wiki/2I/Borisov "2I/Borisov") and [3I/ATLAS](/wiki/3I/ATLAS "3I/ATLAS") never orbited the Sun and therefore do not require a 3rd-body interaction to be ejected from the Solar System.

### Extinction

Main article: [Extinct comet](/wiki/Extinct_comet "Extinct comet")

Jupiter-family comets and long-period comets appear to follow very different fading laws. The JFCs are active over a lifetime of about 10,000 years or ~1,000 orbits whereas long-period comets fade much faster. Only 10% of the long-period comets survive more than 50 passages to small perihelion and only 1% of them survive more than 2,000 passages.[[35]](#cite_note-dormant-35) Eventually most of the volatile material contained in a comet nucleus evaporates, and the comet becomes a small, dark, inert lump of rock or rubble that can resemble an asteroid.[[145]](#cite_note-145) Some asteroids in elliptical orbits are now identified as extinct comets.[[146]](#cite_note-146)[[147]](#cite_note-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148)[[149]](#cite_note-149) Roughly six percent of the near-Earth asteroids are thought to be extinct comet nuclei.[[35]](#cite_note-dormant-35)

### Breakup and collisions

The nucleus of some comets may be fragile, a conclusion supported by the observation of comets splitting apart.[[150]](#cite_note-150) A significant cometary disruption was that of [Comet Shoemaker–Levy 9](/wiki/Comet_Shoemaker%E2%80%93Levy_9 "Comet Shoemaker–Levy 9"), which was discovered in 1993. A close encounter in July 1992 had broken it into pieces, and over a period of six days in July 1994, these pieces fell into Jupiter's atmosphere—the first time astronomers had observed a collision between two objects in the Solar System.[[151]](#cite_note-151)[[152]](#cite_note-152) Other splitting comets include [3D/Biela](/wiki/3D/Biela "3D/Biela") in 1846 and [73P/Schwassmann–Wachmann](/wiki/73P/Schwassmann%E2%80%93Wachmann "73P/Schwassmann–Wachmann") from 1995 to 2006.[[153]](#cite_note-spitzer2006-153) Greek historian [Ephorus](/wiki/Ephorus "Ephorus") reported that a comet split apart as far back as the winter of 372–373 [BCE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era").[[154]](#cite_note-great-154) Comets are suspected of splitting due to thermal stress, internal gas pressure, or impact.[[155]](#cite_note-split-155)

Comets [42P/Neujmin](/wiki/42P/Neujmin "42P/Neujmin") and [53P/Van Biesbroeck](/wiki/53P/Van_Biesbroeck "53P/Van Biesbroeck") appear to be fragments of a parent comet. Numerical integrations have shown that both comets had a rather close approach to Jupiter in January 1850, and that, before 1850, the two orbits were nearly identical.[[156]](#cite_note-DPS35-156) Another group of comets that is the result of fragmentation episodes is the Liller comet family made of C/1988 A1 (Liller), C/1996 Q1 (Tabur), C/2015 F3 (SWAN), C/2019 Y1 (ATLAS), and [C/2023 V5 (Leonard)](/wiki/C/2023_V5_(Leonard) "C/2023 V5 (Leonard)").[[157]](#cite_note-Sekanina-2016-157)[[158]](#cite_note-de_la_Fuente_Marcos-2023-158)

Some comets have been observed to break up during their perihelion passage, including great comets [West](/wiki/Comet_West "Comet West") and [Ikeya–Seki](/wiki/Comet_Ikeya%E2%80%93Seki "Comet Ikeya–Seki"). Biela's Comet was one significant example when it broke into two pieces during its passage through the perihelion in 1846. These two comets were seen separately in 1852, but never again afterward. Instead, spectacular [meteor showers](/wiki/Meteor_showers "Meteor showers") were seen in 1872 and 1885 when the comet should have been visible. A minor meteor shower, the [Andromedids](/wiki/Andromedids "Andromedids"), occurs annually in November, and it is caused when Earth crosses the orbit of Biela's Comet.[[159]](#cite_note-159)

Some comets meet a more spectacular end – either falling into the Sun[[160]](#cite_note-160) or colliding with a planet or other body. Collisions between comets and planets or moons were common in the early Solar System: some of the many craters on the Moon, for example, may have been caused by comets. A recent collision of a comet with a planet occurred in July 1994 when Comet Shoemaker–Levy 9 broke up into pieces and collided with Jupiter.[[161]](#cite_note-161)
